description:
You act as the artifact-manager of an on-premise binary repository that stores pre-compiled
optimization solvers.  Your goal is to create a *curation plan* that tells the operations
team which artefacts can be deleted to free disk space while keeping the most recent
version of every solver.

Repository location (read-only for you):
/home/user/repos/solvers

1. Inside that directory you will find several binary archives whose file names follow
   this exact pattern

      <solver-name>-<version>-linux-x86_64.<ext>

   • <solver-name>    is a lower-case ASCII identifier without dashes    
   • <version>        is either MAJOR.MINOR or MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH  
   • <ext>            is tar.gz or bin

2. Create the directory
      /home/user/reports
   if it does not yet exist (it must end up world-readable, i.e. mode 755).

3. Produce the machine-readable file
      /home/user/reports/curation_plan.json
   obeying the *exact* JSON schema shown below.  
   • **Keys must appear in precisely the order** given.  
   • The file must be minified (no unnecessary whitespace or newlines except the single
     newline that closes the file).

   Schema with example values (do **not** hard-code them; detect dynamically):

   {
     "repository": "/home/user/repos/solvers",
     "generated_at": "2023-06-18T12:59:31Z",
     "solvers": [
       {
         "name": "glpk",
         "kept_version": "5.1",
         "removed": [
           { "version": "5.0",
             "path": "glpk-5.0-linux-x86_64.tar.gz",
             "size_bytes": 1536000 }
         ],
         "reclaimed_bytes": 1536000
       }
       /* additional solver objects in alphabetical order … */
     ],
     "total_reclaimed_bytes": 595456000
   }

   Required details:
   • "generated_at" MUST be in RFC-3339 UTC (yyyy-mm-ddThh:mm:ssZ).  
   • In every solver object
        - "kept_version" is the numerically newest version string you found.  
        - The "removed" array lists *all* older versions *in ascending version order*.  
        - "reclaimed_bytes" is the sum of size_bytes for that solver.  
   • "solvers" array itself has to be sorted alphabetically by solver name.  
   • "total_reclaimed_bytes" is the sum of all reclaimed_bytes values.  

4. Produce the human log
      /home/user/reports/curation_plan.log

   It MUST consist of exactly one line per solver, followed by one final total line,
   each terminated by ‘\n’.  
   Format of the lines (values separated by a single space, no commas):

     <solver-name> reclaimed <bytes> bytes by removing <n> file(s)

   Example:

     glpk reclaimed 1536000 bytes by removing 1 file(s)
     …
     Total reclaimed 595456000 bytes

5. Do *not* move or delete any artefact.  Only create the two report files.

You can use any standard Linux shell utilities that are available in a typical Ubuntu
installation.  Everything must be doable in no more than 10 reasonably short commands
(e.g. ls, awk, sed, jq, date -u ― long pipelines count as one command).

When you are done, both /home/user/reports/curation_plan.json and
/home/user/reports/curation_plan.log must exist and exactly meet the format rules
above—automated tests will compare them byte-for-byte.
truth:
Initial repository state — files already present before the agent starts:

Directory: /home/user/repos/solvers  (mode 755)

1. glpk-5.0-linux-x86_64.tar.gz       size  1536000  bytes
2. glpk-5.1-linux-x86_64.tar.gz       size  1658880  bytes
3. cplex-12.10-linux-x86_64.bin       size 307200000 bytes
4. cplex-22.1-linux-x86_64.bin        size 319979520 bytes
5. gurobi-9.1.1-linux-x86_64.tar.gz   size 286720000 bytes
6. gurobi-10.0.0-linux-x86_64.tar.gz  size 325058560 bytes

The correct curation output the tests will look for:

File 1: /home/user/reports/curation_plan.json (minified, followed by a single NL)

{"repository":"/home/user/repos/solvers","generated_at":"REGEX_ISO_TIMESTAMP","solvers":[{"name":"cplex","kept_version":"22.1","removed":[{"version":"12.10","path":"cplex-12.10-linux-x86_64.bin","size_bytes":307200000}],"reclaimed_bytes":307200000},{"name":"glpk","kept_version":"5.1","removed":[{"version":"5.0","path":"glpk-5.0-linux-x86_64.tar.gz","size_bytes":1536000}],"reclaimed_bytes":1536000},{"name":"gurobi","kept_version":"10.0.0","removed":[{"version":"9.1.1","path":"gurobi-9.1.1-linux-x86_64.tar.gz","size_bytes":286720000}],"reclaimed_bytes":286720000}],"total_reclaimed_bytes":595456000}

• The tests allow any value that matches
  ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$
  to appear in "generated_at".

• Exact byte count of the expected json file (with real timestamp) will vary because
  of the timestamp, so the harness verifies:
  – valid JSON,  
  – key order, content & integer values exactly as above,  
  – correct RFC-3339 timestamp.

File 2: /home/user/reports/curation_plan.log

cplex reclaimed 307200000 bytes by removing 1 file(s)
glpk  reclaimed 1536000 bytes by removing 1 file(s)
gurobi reclaimed 286720000 bytes by removing 1 file(s)
Total reclaimed 595456000 bytes

(Each line ends with '\n'.  Two spaces after “glpk” so that all columns align is *not*
required; tests will trim consecutive whitespace.)

Both files must be world-readable (mode 644) and located inside a directory that is
world-readable/executable (mode 755).

No other files or directories have to be created.
